text: The surgeon enters the lesser sac, revealing the visible contours of the gastrointestinal stromal tumor (GIST) along the lesser curve of the stomach.
The surgeon uses a Synchro Seal instrument, which is comparable in size to a Maryland dissector, to perform precise dissection and lymphadenectomy.

The surgeon uses the third arm to suture the greater curvature of the stomach to the anterior wall, effectively suspending the greater curve to expose the tumor located on the posterior wall of the stomach.
The surgeon uses the synchro seal instrument to dissect through the stomach tissue, providing a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses silk sutures to assist with tissue retraction, providing better exposure of the surgical field.
The surgeon views the posterior wall of the stomach, examining its anatomical features and spatial orientation.
The surgeon begins suturing using a V-Loc suture device, preparing to secure the tissue.
The surgical field shows good blood flow in the area being operated on.
The surgical field shows active bleeding from the tissue.
The duodenum is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs an up-down closure, aligning the tissue edges vertically to complete the closure process.
The surgeon bisects the oval-shaped area that was initially created, preparing to close it horizontally.
The surgeon completes a single-layer closure of the surgical site, following the previous vertical and horizontal closure techniques.
The stomach appears bruised and manipulated, likely due to the removal of a V-lock suture.
The surgeon focuses on the second area adjacent to the incisura, highlighting the specific part of the surgical site being addressed.
The surgeon begins closing the incision from right to left to avoid narrowing the area adjacent to the incisura.
The video shows narrowing at the level of the pylorus, indicating a constricted area in the stomach near the pyloric region.
The surgeon performs a Heineke-Mikulicz pyloroplasty to widen the narrowed area at the pylorus, visibly opening the constricted region.
The surgeon pulls on a suture located just behind the pylorus, visibly manipulating the tissue in that area.
The surgeon holds the scope at the level of the pylorus, where narrowing is observed.
The surgeon begins extending the incision, creating a five-centimeter opening in the stomach and a two-centimeter opening in the duodenum.
The surgeon performs a myotomy using scissors, cutting through the muscle layer to create an incision.
The surgeon switches the camera from the third trocar to the second trocar to improve visibility of the stomach area.
The surgeon closes the pyloroplasty in one layer using a V-lock suture, visibly securing the tissue edges together.
